awd · Booklet

Union Types and State Management

← Back to Course Package

Chapter: Union Types and State Management

Introduction

In modern software development, managing data that can change or take on multiple forms is one of the most significant challenges. Whether you are building a complex web application or a simple terminal-based number guessing game, you must distinguish between what stays the same and what changes. This chapter introduces the foundational concepts of state management in TypeScript, focusing on how to handle unpredictable user input using Union Types and Type Guards. By the end of this reading, you will understand how to configure a modern TypeScript project, differentiate between configuration and mutable state, and use type narrowing to write safer, more predictable code.

Core Concepts

To build robust applications, we must first categorize our data. In our guessing game example, we deal with two primary types of data: Configuration and Mutable State.

ConceptDescriptionKeyword
ConfigurationStatic values that remain immutable throughout the execution, such as a secret target number.const
Mutable StateOperational status that changes over time, such as whether a game loop should continue.let

Beyond simple variables, TypeScript provides powerful tools for handling flexibility:

  • Union Types: Created using the pipe operator (|), these allow a variable to hold one of several specified types. For example, number | string.
  • Type Guard: A runtime check (like typeof) that allows the TypeScript compiler to ensure a variable is a specific type before performing operations on it.
  • Type Narrowing: The process where TypeScript identifies a more specific type for a variable within a conditional branch, moving from a broad union to a specific type.

How It Works

Let us examine the implementation of these concepts through a number guessing game. The process begins with project configuration and moves into logic implementation.

1. Project Configuration

Modern development favors ECMAScript Modules (ESM). To set this up, we avoid the bloated defaults of tsc --init. Instead, we manually configure our package.json to include "type": "module". This ensures our project aligns with modern standards. Our dependencies typically include typescript and @types/node to provide the necessary environment for our code to run.

2. Handling Flexible Input with Union Types

In a guessing game, a user might type a number to guess, or they might type the word "quit" to exit. We declare a variable that can handle both using a Union Type:

let playerGuess: number | string;

When we receive input, we assign it based on the user's intent:

if (rawInput.toLowerCase() === "quit") {
    playerGuess = "quit";
} else {
    playerGuess = parseFloat(rawInput);
}

3. Implementing Type Guards and Narrowing

TypeScript is protective. It will not let you perform math on playerGuess because it might be a string. To fix this, we use a Type Guard to narrow the type:

if (typeof playerGuess === "number") {
    if (isNaN(playerGuess)) {
        console.log("That doesn't look like a valid number");
    } else {
        const difference = Math.abs(playerGuess - secretNumber);
        if (difference === 0) {
            console.log("You win!");
            isGameRunning = false;
        }
    }
} else if (playerGuess === "quit") {
    console.log("Goodbye!");
    isGameRunning = false;
}

Inside the first if block, TypeScript knows playerGuess is definitely a number. This allows us to calculate the difference safely. Note the use of const difference inside the block; this is block scoping, which keeps our variables local to where they are needed and prevents global scope pollution.

Common Mistakes & Gotchas

  • The Math Trap: Students often think they can perform arithmetic on a union type immediately. Actually, TypeScript will throw an error if you try to perform math on number | string before using a type guard to prove it is a number.
  • The NaN Oversight: Students often assume parseFloat() guarantees a usable number. Actually, parseFloat() can return NaN (Not a Number) even if the variable type is technically number, requiring an isNaN() check for true validation.
  • Config Bloat: Students often use tsc --init to start projects. Actually, this creates a bloated config file; manually providing a slimmed-down tsconfig.json is often cleaner and more educational for beginners.
  • Module Errors: Students often forget the package.json settings. Actually, forgetting to set "type": "module" will cause issues when trying to use modern ESM syntax in a Node environment.

Summary

  • Use const for configuration and let for mutable state like loop flags.
  • Union types (|) allow variables to hold multiple potential types safely.
  • Type guards (typeof) enable type narrowing, allowing specific logic to run on specific types.
  • Always validate numeric input with isNaN() even after type narrowing.
  • Modern TypeScript projects require "type": "module" in package.json for ESM support.
